name: Halliburton Contracts
description: The Halliburton Contracts refer to a series of no-bid or limited-bid contracts awarded to Halliburton, an American multinational corporation providing services to the oil and gas industry, and specifically its subsidiary Kellogg Brown & Root (KBR), primarily during the Iraq War and its aftermath (2003-2011). Conspiracy theories and criticisms allege that these contracts were awarded due to Vice President Dick Cheney's prior role as CEO of Halliburton (1995-2000), suggesting cronyism, corruption, and war profiteering. Proponents point to the sheer scale of the contracts (billions of dollars), the lack of competitive bidding processes, and instances of alleged overcharging and poor performance by KBR as evidence of wrongdoing. They suggest a deliberate plan to enrich Halliburton at the expense of taxpayers and soldiers. Mainstream perspectives acknowledge that Halliburton received substantial contracts but argue that this was due to their existing infrastructure, logistical capabilities, and experience in providing necessary services in a challenging environment. Government investigations, while finding instances of overcharging and mismanagement, have largely attributed these to bureaucratic errors, unforeseen circumstances, and the difficulties of operating in a war zone rather than a deliberate conspiracy. Critics of the conspiracy theories also highlight the necessity of rapid response and infrastructure support during wartime, which may have justified expedited contracting procedures. The debate continues regarding the fairness, transparency, and ultimate cost-effectiveness of the contracts awarded to Halliburton.
subtitle: Controversial contracts in the Iraq War.

claims.claim: Halliburton received preferential treatment due to Dick Cheney's prior role.
claims.evidence: Cheney's previous position as CEO, large no-bid contracts awarded.
claims.criticism: Halliburton's pre-existing infrastructure and capabilities made them a logical choice.

claims.claim: Halliburton overcharged the government for services.
claims.evidence: Reports of exorbitant fees for fuel and other supplies.
claims.criticism: Overcharging was often due to logistical challenges, security costs, and unforeseen circumstances in a war zone. Some overcharges were repaid.

claims.claim: Halliburton's services were substandard and wasteful.
claims.evidence: Reports of poorly constructed facilities and inefficient operations.
claims.criticism: Operating in a war zone presented extreme challenges, and some inefficiencies were unavoidable. Government oversight was also a factor.

keyFigures.name: Dick Cheney
keyFigures.role: Former CEO of Halliburton, US Vice President

keyFigures.name: Bunnatine Greenhouse
keyFigures.role: Former Army Corps of Engineers contracting official who testified about irregularities

keyFigures.name: Pratap Chatterjee
keyFigures.role: Journalist and author critical of Halliburton's contracts

timeline.event: Dick Cheney serves as CEO of Halliburton.
Start of the Iraq War and awarding of large contracts to Halliburton/KBR.
Public scrutiny and congressional investigations into Halliburton contracts begin.
End of the Iraq War and gradual winding down of Halliburton's contracts.

sources.author: Pratap Chatterjee
sources.work: Halliburton's Army: How A Well-Connected Oil Company Revolutionized The Way America Makes War
sources.title: Pratap Chatterjee

sources.author: Allison Stanger
sources.work: One Nation Under Contract: The Outsourcing of American Power and the Future of Foreign Policy
sources.title: Allison Stanger
